scenario: Show which part of result matched

v1_junior.what_still_breaks: User doesn't see why match

v2_mid_level.what_still_breaks: dangerouslySetInnerHTML = XSS, no fuzzy match highlights, regex chars break

v3_senior.code: // Safe highlighting via text splitting (no innerHTML) function Highlight({text,query}){const parts=text.split(new RegExp(`(${escapeRegex(query)})`,'gi'));return <>{parts.map((p,i)=>p.toLowerCase()===query.toLowerCase()?{p}:{p})}}
v3_senior.what_makes_it_senior: Escape regex chars, no XSS, React-native

v4_staff.what_makes_it_staff: Server-provided ranges (correct for fuzzy/stemming), client just renders

v5_principal.code:
// Highlighting communicates relevance. Beyond marks: snippet windows (excerpt around match), field-specific (matched in title vs body), score visualization.
// Search products design this carefully — Algolia/Elastic UIs are exemplars.
v5_principal.the_realization: Highlighting is the explanation of search ranking. Done well, users understand why results matched and can refine. Done poorly, results feel random. Server-side ranges + thoughtful snippet windows are the difference between search-as-toy and search-as-product.

when_to_use_which_version: v3 minimum, v4-v5 for serious search

v1_junior.what_still_breaks: User can override, no size check, no count limit

v2_mid_level.what_still_breaks: After-selection error (annoying), MIME spoofable, alerts gross

v3_senior.code:
// Validate magic bytes (real file type)
async function detectType(file){const buf=await file.slice(0,12).arrayBuffer();const arr=new Uint8Array(buf);if(arr[0]===0xFF&&arr[1]===0xD8)return 'image/jpeg';if(arr[0]===0x89&&arr[1]===0x50)return 'image/png';...}
v3_senior.what_makes_it_senior: Magic-byte detection (security), inline error states, multiple files awareness

v4_staff.code:
// File System Access API picker with constraints (Chrome+)
const handles=await window.showOpenFilePicker({types:[{description:'Images',accept:{'image/*':['.jpg','.png']}}],excludeAcceptAllOption:true,multiple:true})
v4_staff.what_makes_it_staff: Real OS picker, type-filtered at OS level, multiple selection

v5_principal.code: // Security: client validation is UX, not security. Server must re-validate magic bytes, scan for malware, sanitize SVG/PDF. Client validation prevents user friction; server validation prevents attacks.
v5_principal.the_realization: File constraints have two layers: UX (fast feedback, friendly) and security (server, authoritative). Engineers conflate them — trusting client validation creates vulnerabilities; making server the only check creates terrible UX. Both, layered.

when_to_use_which_version: v3 minimum, v4 modern Chrome, v5 always think security

v1_junior.what_still_breaks: All images load immediately, bandwidth wasted

v2_mid_level.what_still_breaks: No placeholder, no priority for above-fold, no fade-in

v3_senior.what_makes_it_senior: Priority awareness, decoding hint, native primitives over JS

v4_staff.what_makes_it_staff: Native lazy + LQIP + smooth fade, no JS observer needed

v5_principal.code:
// content-visibility:auto for not-yet-visible content blocks (browser skips layout). Combine with lazy-load — better LCP, better INP. Often eliminates need for virtualization in moderate lists.
.gallery > article{content-visibility:auto;contain-intrinsic-size:300px}
v5_principal.the_realization: Lazy loading was JS in 2018. Now: native loading=lazy, content-visibility, fetchpriority, decoding=async cover most cases. Reaching for IntersectionObserver is often wasteful when the platform has it. Knowing the platform = leverage.

when_to_use_which_version: v2 minimum, v3+ standard

scenario: Where does the drag preview appear relative to cursor

v1_junior.what_still_breaks: Cursor hovers over preview, confusing

v2_mid_level.what_still_breaks: Hard-coded offset, doesn't account for click position

v3_senior.code:
// Capture click position relative to item, offset drag image to maintain
const rect=node.getBoundingClientRect();const offsetX=e.clientX-rect.left;const offsetY=e.clientY-rect.top;e.dataTransfer.setDragImage(node,offsetX,offsetY)
v3_senior.what_makes_it_senior: Natural feel — preview stays under finger/cursor where user grabbed

v4_staff.code:
// dnd-kit auto-handles this; DragOverlay maintains relative position
<DndContext><DragOverlay>{...}</DragOverlay></DndContext>
v4_staff.what_makes_it_staff: Library handles, scaling/rotation transforms possible during drag

v5_principal.code:
// Drag preview is microinteraction. Subtle scale, shadow, slight rotation communicate 'lifted'. Drop = animate back into place. Polish: physics-based release.
// Apple's iOS drag-drop is the gold standard.
v5_principal.the_realization: Drag micro-interactions are the difference between 'it works' and 'it feels alive'. Scale on lift, shadow on hover, snap on drop — pennies of CSS that change perceived quality. Engineers ignore; designers obsess.

when_to_use_which_version: v3 always, v4-v5 with library

v1_junior.what_still_breaks: Can't dismiss, can't update without deploy, no targeting

v2_mid_level.what_still_breaks: No dismissal memory, shows on every load, no audience targeting

v3_senior.code:
// Dismissible with ID memory
{banner&&!dismissed.has(banner.id)&&<Banner onDismiss={()=>{dismissed.add(banner.id);localStorage.setItem('dismissed',JSON.stringify([...dismissed]))}}>
v3_senior.what_makes_it_senior: Per-banner dismiss, localStorage memory, doesn't re-show after dismiss

v4_staff.code:
// Audience targeting (admin only, paid users, region), severity (info/warn/critical = non-dismissible), scheduling (show during window)
const {banners}=useQuery('/banners',{select:bs=>bs.filter(matchAudience(user))})
v4_staff.what_makes_it_staff: Multi-banner, targeted, scheduled, severity-coded

v5_principal.code:
// In-app messaging system: banners + tooltips + announcements + emails + push, all from one CMS. Marketers/PM update without engineering. Track engagement.
// Tools: Beamer, Frigade, Pendo, in-house
v5_principal.the_realization: Banners are one channel in an in-app messaging system. Built as one-off → constant deploys. Built as system → marketing/PM can run campaigns. The architecture investment unlocks org velocity.

when_to_use_which_version: v3 minimum, v4-v5 for serious products

v1_junior.what_still_breaks: OIDC has 12 edge cases, SAML is XML horror, security implementations wrong

v2_mid_level.code:
// passport.js / Auth.js
import NextAuth from 'next-auth';// Supports Google, GitHub, Okta, etc.
v2_mid_level.what_still_breaks: No SAML, no SCIM, multi-tenant SSO config painful

v3_senior.code:
// WorkOS / Auth0 / Clerk for enterprise SSO (SAML + OIDC + SCIM provisioning)
const session=await workos.sso.getSessionFromCode({clientId,code})
v3_senior.what_makes_it_senior: Enterprise-grade, SAML+OIDC+SCIM, audit logs, multi-tenant

v4_staff.code: // Just-in-time provisioning (auto-create user from SSO attrs), domain capture (any @company.com auto-routes to their IDP), JIT permission mapping from groups
v4_staff.what_makes_it_staff: Enterprise onboarding minimal, attribute-mapped roles, domain-based routing

v5_principal.code:
// SSO is a sales feature. SOC2, role mapping, audit logs, IDP-initiated, RBAC integration — all required to close enterprise deals. Auth is product, not plumbing.
// 'SSO Tax' is a real revenue model.
v5_principal.the_realization: SSO isn't 'a login method' — it's the gate for enterprise sales. SOC2 audits, SCIM provisioning, attribute mapping, audit logs. Without it, you don't sell to Fortune 500. With it, you charge 2-10x. Auth is revenue.

when_to_use_which_version: v3 for B2B, v4-v5 for enterprise

v1_junior.improvements: Next improvement for Match user's OS theme automatically: extract the invariant into a tested boundary, then add observability and rollout controls.
v1_junior.what_still_breaks: Mac users in dark mode get blinded

v2_mid_level.what_still_breaks: No override, no JS coordination, no FOUC handling

v3_senior.what_makes_it_senior: Three-state (auto/light/dark), live OS sync, override persists

v4_staff.code:
// Inline script in <head> applies theme before paint (no FOUC)
<script>{`(function(){const t=localStorage.getItem('theme')||(matchMedia('(prefers-color-scheme:dark)').matches?'dark':'light');document.documentElement.dataset.theme=t})()`}</script>
v4_staff.what_makes_it_staff: Pre-paint theme application, hydration-safe

v5_principal.code:
// Theme is more than light/dark. prefers-reduced-motion, prefers-contrast, prefers-reduced-transparency, prefers-color-scheme: forced. Stack them.
// Apple Health, Google Photos coordinate multiple a11y prefs.
v5_principal.the_realization: OS preferences are user signals. Honoring them is the baseline; coordinating multiple (color + contrast + motion + transparency) is the modern accessibility floor. Engineers ship dark mode and call it done; users need the full set.

when_to_use_which_version: v3 always, v4-v5 for design systems

v1_junior.what_still_breaks: Ugly URLs, no SEO, no SSR

v2_mid_level.what_still_breaks: Server must serve index.html for all routes, otherwise 404 on refresh

v3_senior.what_makes_it_senior: SPA fallback configured, refresh works

v4_staff.code:
// SSR: each route renders server-side
// Next.js / Remix / TanStack Router. No SPA fallback needed — every URL is a real page.
v4_staff.what_makes_it_staff: Real URLs, SEO, no SPA-fallback hack, server-side data

v5_principal.code:
// Hash routing has one valid use case: static-host-only (S3) or anchor-based navigation. Otherwise: always history. Choose framework based on rendering need (CSR/SSR/SSG/RSC).
// View Transitions API makes SSR feel SPA-fast.
v5_principal.the_realization: Hash routing was a workaround for browser limits. History API + server config solves it. Modern frameworks (Next/Remix) make history routing zero-effort. Engineers reaching for hash now haven't reset their assumptions since 2015.

when_to_use_which_version: v3 minimum, v4 for serious apps

scenario: Convert technical errors to helpful text

v1_junior.code: toast(error.message);// e.g., 'NetworkError when attempting to fetch resource'
v1_junior.what_still_breaks: Technical, no action, scary

v2_mid_level.code:
// Hardcoded mapping
const msg={NetworkError:'Connection problem',TimeoutError:'Request took too long'}[error.name]||'Something went wrong'
v2_mid_level.what_still_breaks: No action, no i18n, no remediation

v3_senior.what_makes_it_senior: Errors include remediation, user message at error-creation site

v4_staff.what_makes_it_staff: i18n-ready, parametrized, translation pipeline aware

v5_principal.code:
// Errors as product. PM writes the error copy. UX designer picks the pattern (toast/page/inline). Each error gets: code, copy, severity, action, recovery flow.
// Linear's error UX is excellent — review their patterns.
v5_principal.the_realization: Error copy is product writing. 'Something went wrong' loses trust; 'We couldn't save — your connection dropped, try again' keeps it. Engineers write errors; product teams design them. The latter retains users.

when_to_use_which_version: v3 always, v4-v5 for SaaS

v1_junior.improvements: Next improvement for Catch layout shifts: extract the invariant into a tested boundary, then add observability and rollout controls.
v1_junior.what_still_breaks: Shifts annoy users, Google penalty

v2_mid_level.what_still_breaks: Async content (ads, embeds), font-load shifts, dynamic content

v3_senior.code:
// web-vitals onCLS with attribution
import {onCLS} from 'web-vitals/attribution';onCLS(({value,attribution})=>send({cls:value,largestShiftSource:attribution.largestShiftSource}))
v3_senior.what_makes_it_senior: Identifies which element shifted (attribution), actionable data

v4_staff.what_makes_it_staff: Comprehensive prevention, modern CSS, font fallback metrics matched

v5_principal.code:
// CLS is a discipline problem. Every dynamic content needs reserved space. Skeleton screens prevent shifts. Layout-altering API calls deferred or reserved.
// CI gates on CLS regression per route. Visual regression tests catch shifts.
v5_principal.the_realization: CLS is the most fixable Web Vital — almost all shifts are preventable with discipline (reserve space, aspect-ratio, font matching). Engineers tolerate shifts; design systems eliminate them. CLS = quality signal.

when_to_use_which_version: v3 always, v4-v5 systematic

v1_junior.what_still_breaks: No loading, error, race conditions, no cancellation, refetch on every render

v2_mid_level.what_still_breaks: No cache, no dedup, no revalidation, no abort, no SSR

v3_senior.code:
// AbortController, cache by url, dedup in-flight
const cache=new Map();function useFetch(url){const [state,setState]=useState({...});useEffect(()=>{if(cache.has(url)){setState({data:cache.get(url),loading:false});return}const c=new AbortController();fetch(url,{signal:c.signal})...;return()=>c.abort()},[url])}
v3_senior.what_makes_it_senior: Abort, cache, dedup — but reinventing React Query

v4_staff.code:
// Don't write useFetch. Use React Query / SWR. They've solved cache, dedup, revalidation, retry, SSR, optimistic, etc.
useQuery({queryKey:[url],queryFn:()=>fetch(url).then(r=>r.json())})
v4_staff.what_makes_it_staff: Recognize when to use library; useFetch is anti-pattern

v5_principal.code:
// Fetching evolves: useEffect → useFetch → React Query → Server Components. Each layer removes work. RSC eliminates client-side fetching for many cases.
// Pattern: server-first, client-fallback.
v5_principal.the_realization: useFetch is a rite of passage and a trap. Every team writes one; every team adds caching, then dedup, then retry, then realizes they've built React Query badly. Use the library. Move on to product.

when_to_use_which_version: avoid v3; use library immediately

scenario: User adds/removes rows (e.g., line items in invoice)

v1_junior.what_still_breaks: No keys (uses index), reorder breaks state, no validation per row

v2_mid_level.what_still_breaks: No client-side row totals, no shortcuts (Tab in last row = new row), no virtualization

v3_senior.code:
// Total computed reactively, Tab+Enter add row, Esc removes empty row
const total=fields.reduce((s,f)=>s+f.qty*f.price,0);onKeyDown:(e,i)=>{if(e.key==='Enter'&&i===fields.length-1)append({})}
v3_senior.what_makes_it_senior: Keyboard shortcuts, computed totals, smart defaults

v4_staff.code:
// Virtualized rows for many items (invoice with 500 lines)
const rv=useVirtualizer({count:fields.length,...})
v4_staff.what_makes_it_staff: Performance at scale, keyboard nav across virtualized rows

v5_principal.code:
// Field arrays are spreadsheet-shaped UIs. Eventually users want: paste from Excel, formulas, computed columns. Build that or use AG-Grid/Handsontable.
// Recognize when a form is actually a spreadsheet.
v5_principal.the_realization: Dynamic field arrays in forms become miniature spreadsheets. When users want paste, formulas, multi-select — you've built a spreadsheet badly. Use the right tool: Handsontable for actual spreadsheets, form library for short lists.

when_to_use_which_version: v2-v3 for forms, v4-v5 for spreadsheet-like

scenario: Select items on page 1, navigate to page 2, then back

v1_junior.what_still_breaks: Frustrating UX, user re-selects

v2_mid_level.what_still_breaks: Lost when leaving feature, no cross-page bulk actions

v3_senior.code:
// Selection in URL params or session storage
// + Visible 'X selected' banner with bulk actions
v3_senior.what_makes_it_senior: Persists, visible feedback, bulk actions surface

v4_staff.code:
// 'Select all matching filter' (not just visible). 'You selected 1,247 items across 13 pages.'
const {selectAll}=useSelection();// selectAll = filter, not list of IDs
v4_staff.what_makes_it_staff: Scale-aware selection (filter-based, not enumeration), bulk on huge datasets

v5_principal.code:
// Bulk selection is operations design. 'Apply tag to 10,000 items' — should be async job with progress, not synchronous request. Backend processes; frontend tracks job.
// Gmail's 'Select all 1,247' is the pattern.
v5_principal.the_realization: Selection at scale is a job scheduling problem disguised as UI. 10 items: sync. 10,000 items: async, queued, tracked, notified. Engineers ship 'loop through and update'; users hit timeouts and rage-quit.

when_to_use_which_version: v3 minimum, v4-v5 for serious tools

scenario: Modal opens with video; close pauses

v1_junior.what_still_breaks: Closing keeps playing (memory leak), no pause on hidden tab, autoplay blocked

v2_mid_level.what_still_breaks: Audio in tab when navigating elsewhere keeps playing, no PiP

v3_senior.code:
// Visibility-aware
useEffect(()=>{const h=()=>{if(document.hidden)videoRef.current?.pause()};document.addEventListener('visibilitychange',h);return()=>document.removeEventListener('visibilitychange',h)},[])
v3_senior.what_makes_it_senior: Tab visibility respected, autoplay handled (muted+playsinline for iOS), cleanup

v4_staff.what_makes_it_staff: PiP, OS integration (lock screen controls), accessibility

v5_principal.code:
// Media in modals = product decision. Videos that should persist (background music, tutorials) belong outside modals. Modal is for content review/preview.
// Treat media as first-class state, not modal-internal.
v5_principal.the_realization: Media interrupts gracefully or ungracefully. Modal-trapped video disappears with close; PiP-aware video continues. The choice is UX intent (preview vs consume). Engineers default to in-modal and break on close; product designs the continuation.

when_to_use_which_version: v3 always, v4-v5 for media products

scenario: Group rows by column, collapsible groups

v1_junior.code:
// Group manually
const grouped=groupBy(rows,'status');// Render <section> per group
v1_junior.what_still_breaks: No collapse, no count per group, no aggregations (sum, avg)

v2_mid_level.what_still_breaks: State per group lost, no virtualization, no grand total

v3_senior.code:
// TanStack Table grouping
const tbl=useReactTable({columns,data,getGroupedRowModel:getGroupedRowModel(),initialState:{grouping:['status']},getExpandedRowModel:getExpandedRowModel()})
v3_senior.what_makes_it_senior: Library handles grouping, expand/collapse, aggregations

v4_staff.what_makes_it_staff: Hierarchical groups, aggregations cascade, server-side optional

v5_principal.code:
// Grouping is pivot table thinking. Eventually users want: pivot by X, aggregate Y, filter Z. That's a BI tool, not a list.
// Recognize when 'fancy table' becomes 'tableau'.
v5_principal.the_realization: Grouping is on the slope to pivots, which is on the slope to BI. Engineers add grouping feature-by-feature; users want the full pivot experience. Embed a real tool (Tableau, Metabase, Hex) or build the table; halfway is worst.

when_to_use_which_version: v3 minimum, v4-v5 for data products

v1_junior.code: // Checkboxes; rerun search on every change
v1_junior.what_still_breaks: Layout shifts when results change count, no facet counts, no clear-all

v2_mid_level.what_still_breaks: Counts wrong (don't update with other filters), checkbox cascade slow

v3_senior.what_makes_it_senior: Facets calculated per query, counts always accurate

v4_staff.code:
// URL-syncing, deep linking, breadcrumbs of active filters
const params=new URLSearchParams();selected.brands.forEach(b=>params.append('brand',b))
v4_staff.what_makes_it_staff: Shareable filtered URLs, breadcrumb removal of individual filters

v5_principal.code:
// E-commerce search converges on patterns: Amazon's left sidebar, Airbnb's chip filters, Booking.com's modal — each optimal for category. Study category leaders.
// AI-augmented: 'find cheap restaurants for vegetarians near me' → applies filters semantically.
v5_principal.the_realization: Search filters are conversion levers. Counts, facets, URL state, breadcrumbs — each adds percent. AI-driven natural language filtering is the next frontier. Engineers ship checkboxes; product teams analyze category leaders and optimize.

when_to_use_which_version: v3 minimum, v4-v5 for commerce/search

v1_junior.what_still_breaks: No info, can't cancel, no ETA

v2_mid_level.what_still_breaks: Misleading (jumps in chunks), stops near end (server processing), no ETA, no cancel

v3_senior.code:
// Smoothed progress + ETA
const {smoothed,eta}=smoothProgress(pct,time);// Linear interpolation, calculate ETA from rate
v3_senior.what_makes_it_senior: Smooth, ETA, cancellable, indeterminate when server-processing

v4_staff.code:
// Multi-stage: 'Uploading...' (0-90%), 'Processing...' (90-100%), 'Done'
// Server pushes status via SSE/WS
v4_staff.what_makes_it_staff: Realistic phases, server-driven, user understands what's happening

v5_principal.code:
// Progress UI is hope management. Jumpy bars feel broken; predictable phases feel reliable. Apple's Time Machine has the gold standard ETA.
// Honesty over optimism: 'about 2 minutes' is fine; '0:00' looping is hostile.
v5_principal.the_realization: Progress bars are psychological. Smooth, honest, phased reduces anxiety. Jumpy or stuck increases it. Engineers ship raw percentages; designers craft the perception of progress.

when_to_use_which_version: v3 minimum, v4-v5 for serious uploads

scenario: Serve AVIF/WebP to browsers that support; JPEG fallback

v1_junior.what_still_breaks: Larger files than necessary

v2_mid_level.what_still_breaks: Manual format conversion, multiple URLs to manage

v3_senior.what_makes_it_senior: Single URL, CDN handles formats, cleaner code

v4_staff.code:
// On-demand transformation: ?w=400&fmt=auto&q=80
// Cloudinary, imgix, Vercel Image, Cloudflare Images
v4_staff.what_makes_it_staff: Format + resize + crop + watermark per request, CDN-cached

v5_principal.code:
// Images = bandwidth. AVIF saves 30-50% vs JPEG. At scale, that's millions of dollars. Optimization isn't 'good practice' — it's business cost.
// Track: median image size per page, P95, regression on PR.
v5_principal.the_realization: Image bytes are revenue. Fast-loading pages convert better; bandwidth costs less. AVIF/WebP, srcset, content negotiation all compound to measurable conversion lift. Engineers think 'optimization'; finance thinks 'COGS'.

when_to_use_which_version: v2 minimum, v4-v5 for serious traffic

scenario: Drag from sidebar list to canvas drop area

v1_junior.what_still_breaks: Scroll within either zone breaks drag, drop position calculation hard

v2_mid_level.what_still_breaks: Doesn't work across iframes, slow at edges, hard to drag long distances

v3_senior.code:
// dnd-kit auto-scroll plugin
import {createSnapModifier} from '@dnd-kit/modifiers';<DndContext modifiers={[snapToGrid]} autoScroll>
v3_senior.what_makes_it_senior: Library handles scroll, modifiers customize behavior

v4_staff.code:
// Drop = create instance. Sidebar item is template; canvas gets instance. Distinguish 'drop source' (copy) from 'drop sink' (move).
onDrop:(item)=>canvas.add({...template,id:nanoid()})
v4_staff.what_makes_it_staff: Template-instance pattern, copy semantics on drop, multi-instance

v5_principal.code:
// Builder UIs (Figma, Webflow) are sidebars dragging into canvas. The pattern: catalog of templates + canvas of instances + property editor. Three-pane design tool.
// Architecture: instances as JSON tree, canvas renders tree, sidebar palette.
v5_principal.the_realization: Drag-from-sidebar-to-canvas is the visual builder pattern. Once you have that, you're building Figma. Architecture (template/instance, prop editor, undo history) is more important than the drag mechanics.

when_to_use_which_version: v3 minimum, v4-v5 for builder tools

scenario: User controls which emails they receive

v1_junior.what_still_breaks: All-or-nothing, no per-type, no preview

v2_mid_level.what_still_breaks: No frequency (daily/weekly/never), no preview, no example email

v3_senior.code:
// Categories + frequency + preview
<Setting category='digest'><Select value={freq}><Option>Daily</Option><Option>Weekly</Option><Option>Off</Option></Select></Setting>;<Button onClick={showSample}>Preview</Button>
v3_senior.what_makes_it_senior: Frequency choices, examples build confidence, easy off

v4_staff.what_makes_it_staff: Standards compliance, native mail client UX, low friction

v5_principal.code:
// Email is a revenue + relationship surface. Preference center = retention. Granular categories, frequency, account-vs-marketing distinction, GDPR compliance. Bad email programs cause churn.
// Treat preference center as a product.
v5_principal.the_realization: Email preferences look like settings, are actually customer retention. Easy unsubscribe builds trust (paradoxically improves retention). Granular control prevents 'unsubscribe from all' rage-quits. The unsubscribe button is a feature.

when_to_use_which_version: v3 minimum, v4-v5 for SaaS

scenario: Show password strength as user types

v1_junior.code:
// Count: length, has uppercase, has number
if(pwd.length>8&&/[A-Z]/.test(pwd)&&/\d/.test(pwd))setStrong(true)
v1_junior.what_still_breaks: 'Password1' counts as strong, 'correct horse battery staple' as weak. Bad heuristics.

v2_mid_level.code:
// zxcvbn library — entropy-based
import zxcvbn from 'zxcvbn';const result=zxcvbn(pwd);// score 0-4
v2_mid_level.what_still_breaks: Doesn't check against breach DB, no helpful suggestions

v3_senior.code:
// HaveIBeenPwned k-anonymity API: send first 5 chars of SHA1, check if remaining hash in response
const hash=await sha1(pwd);const prefix=hash.slice(0,5);const res=await fetch(`https://api.pwnedpasswords.com/range/${prefix}`);const suffixes=(await res.text()).split('\n');if(suffixes.some(s=>s.startsWith(hash.slice(5))))warn('Password leaked in breach')
v3_senior.what_makes_it_senior: Breach detection (privacy-preserving), entropy meter, helpful suggestions

v4_staff.code:
// Pair with passkey upgrade prompt: 'Want to use Face ID instead? Faster and safer.'
// NIST SP 800-63B compliance: no forced complexity rules, length matters most
v4_staff.what_makes_it_staff: Standards-compliant, passkey-encouraging, friction-minimal

v5_principal.code:
// Password strength meter is consolation prize. Real security: passkeys (no password), passphrases (memorable), breach detection, MFA.
// Eliminate passwords where possible.
v5_principal.the_realization: Strength meters were a 2010 best practice. NIST now advises against complexity rules (force users to bad passwords). Length + breach check + passkey-where-possible is current standard. Engineers are 10 years behind on this.

when_to_use_which_version: v3 minimum, v4-v5 modern auth

v1_junior.what_still_breaks: Inconsistent style, broken links, no color control, slow

v2_mid_level.what_still_breaks: Bundle bloats with imports, no custom icons, tree-shaking spotty

v3_senior.code:
// SVG sprite for app-specific icons; tree-shaken imports for library
<svg><use href='#chevron-right'/></svg>
v3_senior.what_makes_it_senior: One sprite, cached, color via currentColor, tiny payload

v4_staff.what_makes_it_staff: Coherent icon system, scales with text, themed

v5_principal.code:
// Icons are part of brand. Custom set vs library: brand differentiation vs maintenance. Consider: Material Symbols (Google), Lucide (open), or custom commissioned set. Document usage.
// Symbols vs icons: more meaningful, less decorative.
v5_principal.the_realization: Icons signal brand quality. Generic libraries (Heroicons) ship 'reasonable'; custom sets build identity. Stripe, Linear, Notion all have distinct icon vocabularies. The investment shows; the shortcut is invisible (but cheap).

when_to_use_which_version: v3 always, v4-v5 for brand-conscious

v1_junior.improvements: Next improvement for Routes per language (e.g., /en/about, /he/אודות): extract the invariant into a tested boundary, then add observability and rollout controls.
v1_junior.what_still_breaks: Non-English speakers excluded

v2_mid_level.what_still_breaks: Slugs in English (/he/about), no fallback, no SEO hreflang

v3_senior.what_makes_it_senior: Native URLs (SEO, brand), hreflang for search engines

v4_staff.code:
// Auto-detect language from Accept-Language, but persist user choice. URL is single source of truth.
// Negotiation: detect → redirect to localized URL (or show language picker)
v4_staff.what_makes_it_staff: Smart defaults, override-able, deep-linkable to specific language

v5_principal.code:
// i18n is more than translation. Localization (date/number/currency formats), RTL languages, content per region, legal text per jurisdiction. Routes are entry point; content is iceberg.
// Treat as content strategy, not strings.
v5_principal.the_realization: i18n is iceberg work. Routes are surface; translations need workflow, RTL needs CSS audit, regional content needs CMS, legal needs review. Engineers ship translations and miss the system. Localization is product.

when_to_use_which_version: v3 minimum, v4-v5 for serious i18n

scenario: Server rejects form data; show field-level errors

v1_junior.what_still_breaks: Not associated with fields, user has to read error and find field

v2_mid_level.what_still_breaks: Single error per submit, no batch, format varies

v3_senior.what_makes_it_senior: Standard format, multiple errors, focus first error

v4_staff.what_makes_it_staff: Schema parity, type-safe, no drift between client/server validation

v5_principal.code:
// Form is a contract between client and server. tRPC / GraphQL with input validation gives end-to-end types and runtime checks. Errors come back typed, mapped to fields automatically.
// Forms become declarative.
v5_principal.the_realization: Server-validated form errors are where client/server contract becomes visible. Shared schema (Zod) eliminates drift. Without it, errors get out of sync with reality. The frontend trusts what server says — make 'what server says' typed.

when_to_use_which_version: v3 minimum, v4-v5 for serious apps

v1_junior.what_still_breaks: Load fires before app is interactive (data loading, hydration)

v2_mid_level.what_still_breaks: Manual instrumentation, no standard

v3_senior.code:
// INP > TTI in modern thinking. TTI is hard to measure consistently. Focus on INP (response to actual interactions).
import {onINP} from 'web-vitals';onINP(send)
v3_senior.what_makes_it_senior: Modern metric (INP) over outdated (TTI), standardized

v4_staff.code:
// User-Centric Performance Metrics: FCP (visible), LCP (main content), INP (responsive), CLS (stable), TTFB (network).
// Each tells a story; combine.
v4_staff.what_makes_it_staff: Multi-metric view, attribute slowness to phase (network/render/processing)

v5_principal.code:
// Real-user-perceived performance = product. Measure intent (user wanted action) to completion (saw result). Custom marks on key flows. Connect performance to conversion.
// 'Time to first interaction → checkout completion' shows business impact.
v5_principal.the_realization: TTI is a lab metric losing relevance. INP measures real responsiveness. Custom marks measure your product's specific moments. Engineers track web-vitals; product needs flow-specific metrics. Both, layered.

when_to_use_which_version: v3 minimum, v4-v5 for product-level
